subtopics.title: Components
subtopics.content:
- **1** backgammon board (24 narrow triangles called points, numbered 1-24)
- **15** white/light checkers
- **15** black/dark checkers
- **2** pairs of dice (one per player)
- **1** doubling cube (faces showing 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64)
- **2** dice cups

subtopics.title: Board Setup
subtopics.content:
The board has 4 quadrants of 6 points each. Each player sets up their 15 checkers as follows (from that player's perspective):

- **2 checkers** on the 24-point
- **5 checkers** on the 13-point
- **3 checkers** on the 8-point
- **5 checkers** on the 6-point

The bar divides the board into inner (home) and outer boards. Players move in opposite directions.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
- **Determine first player** — Each player rolls one die; higher number goes first, using both numbers rolled.
- **Reroll ties** — If both roll the same number, roll again.
- **Direction** — Players move in opposite directions around the board toward their home board.

subtopics.title: Game Overview
subtopics.content: Backgammon is a two-player race game. Move all 15 of your checkers into your home board, then bear them off. First player to bear off all checkers wins.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
1. **Roll dice** — Roll both dice.
2. **Move checkers** — Use each die value as a separate move. You may move one checker for both dice or two different checkers.
3. **Use both dice** — You must use both numbers if legally possible. If only one can be used, use the higher one.
4. **Doubles** — Rolling doubles gives you 4 moves of that value instead of 2.

#### Moving Rules
- Move checkers forward (toward your home board) by the exact number shown on each die.
- A checker may land on any point that is: empty, occupied by your own checkers, or occupied by exactly 1 opponent checker (a blot).
- A point with 2+ opponent checkers is blocked — you cannot land there.

subtopics.title: Key Actions
subtopics.content:
#### Hitting
- Landing on a point with a single opponent checker (blot) sends it to the bar.
- The hit checker must re-enter from the opponent's home board before any other moves.

#### Entering from the Bar
- Roll dice and enter on a point in the opponent's home board matching a die value (points 1-6 for that player).
- Must enter all checkers from bar before moving any other checker.
- If no legal entry point exists, you lose your turn.

#### Bearing Off
- Once ALL 15 of your checkers are in your home board (points 1-6), you may bear off.
- Use a die roll matching a point number to remove a checker from that point.
- If no checker is on the point rolled, you must make a legal move with a checker on a higher point. If none exist, remove from the highest occupied point.
- If a checker is hit during bearing off, it must re-enter and return to the home board before bearing off resumes.

#### Doubling Cube
- Before rolling, a player may propose to double the stakes.
- Opponent may accept (take) or refuse (drop, losing at current stakes).
- The player who accepted owns the cube and only they can propose the next redouble.

subtopics.title: Special Rules
subtopics.content:
#### Gammon and Backgammon
- **Gammon** — If winner bears off all checkers before opponent bears off any, the game is worth double.
- **Backgammon** — If winner bears off all and opponent still has checkers on the bar or in winner's home board, worth triple.

#### Crawford Rule (Match Play)
- When one player is 1 point from winning the match, the doubling cube cannot be used for that single game.

#### Automatic Doubles (Optional)
- If both players roll the same number on the opening roll, the stakes are automatically doubled.

subtopics.title: Scoring and Endgame
subtopics.content:
#### Winning
- First player to bear off all 15 checkers wins.

#### Match Scoring
- Single game: 1 point × doubling cube value.
- Gammon: 2 points × doubling cube value.
- Backgammon: 3 points × doubling cube value.
- Match is played to a predetermined number of points.

subtopics.title: Beginner Tips
subtopics.content:
- **Make points** — Build consecutive blocked points (a prime) to trap opponent checkers.
- **Don't leave blots** — Avoid leaving single checkers where they can be hit.
- **Secure your home board** — Build points in your home board to make re-entry difficult for opponents.
- **Balance running and blocking** — Pure racing works when ahead; build primes when behind.

subtopics.title: Key Strategies
subtopics.content:
- **Prime strategy** — Build a wall of 6 consecutive points to completely block opponent checkers.
- **Blitz strategy** — Aggressively hit opponent blots and close your home board.
- **Running game** — When ahead in the race (pip count), run your back checkers forward.
- **Back game** — When far behind, hold 2+ points in opponent's home board hoping to hit late.
- **Doubling cube timing** — Offer the cube when you have about 70% winning chances.
